semantic_description: A dedicated edge stitch at the beginning or end of a row that creates a neat, stable selvage for seaming or finishing. The most common selvedge technique is to slip the first stitch of every row purlwise with yarn in back. Not shown in the chart repeat but accounted for in total stitch count. Creates a chain-like edge that is easy to pick up for seaming.

written_forms: slip the first stitch of the row purlwise with yarn in back
selvedge: sl 1 pwise wyib at beginning of row
chain selvedge: sl 1 kwise with yarn in front at beg of row
garter selvedge: knit first and last stitch of every row
ambiguity_notes: 'Selvedge' and 'selvage' are both accepted spellings. In chart form this is usually a visual border mark, not an abbreviation. 'sl 1 wyif' is the most common mechanical instruction for chain selvedge. Different selvedge methods (garter, slip, yarnover) may all be labeled 'selv' in a legend.
Many selvedge methods exist: slip purlwise wyib, slip knitwise, knit first stitch, purl first stitch, garter selvedge. 'Selvedge' alone doesn't specify which technique — check pattern instructions.
Selvedge stitches are often NOT shown in charts but ARE counted in the cast-on number. This causes confusion when counting chart stitches vs. total stitches.

rs: At the beginning of a RS row: slip the first stitch purlwise with yarn in back
At the end of the row: knit or purl the last stitch as specified
This creates one selvedge stitch — repeat at each edge

semantic_description: An applied i-cord that creates a rounded, tube-like edge on the fabric. Instead of a flat selvedge, 3 edge stitches are worked in a continuous i-cord technique: knit 2, knit the 3rd stitch together with the first body stitch (attaching the cord to the fabric). The i-cord stitches are then slid back to the left needle without turning. Creates a polished, professional edge that doesn't require separate finishing.

written_forms: k2, ssk (joining last cord st with first body st), slide 3 sts back to left needle
applied i-cord: knit 3 cord stitches, attach to body by working together with edge
applied i-cord selvedge: k2, ssk (cord + body st), slide 3 back

rs: SSK the 3rd i-cord stitch together with the next body stitch
Slide all 3 i-cord stitches back to left needle (do NOT turn)
